original_abstract: This research investigates the proficiency of Large Language Models (LLMs), specifically GPT-2 and BERT, in representing geometric structures and their corresponding spatial relationships. By encoding geometries from Well-Known Text (WKT) formats into high-dimensional embeddings, the study evaluates the performance of these representations through various downstream tasks, including geometric attribute classification, numeric property regression, and spatial predicate prediction. The experimental results, derived from real-world geospatial datasets, indicate that while LLMs successfully identify geometry types and achieve up to 73% accuracy in predicting spatial predicates, they struggle significantly with precise numeric estimations such as area calculation, centroid derivation, and distance measurement. Furthermore, the models show limited capability in retrieving spatially related objects through textual descriptions. These findings suggest that while LLMs possess foundational knowledge of spatial syntax, current embedding methods—often hindered by coordinate fragmentation and tokenization loss—require the integration of domain-specific geographic knowledge and more sophisticated encoding strategies to effectively support advanced GeoAI applications.
humanized_abstract: Recently, geometric understanding has become one of the key topics in GeoAI, which aims to leverages AI and associated techniques to analyze geographic data. This paper explores the geometric understanding abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-2 and BERT. Through converting Well-Known Text (WKT) representations of different geometries into high-dimensional vectors, their representations and the capabilities of LLMs are verified by using a series of Geo-Knowledge-Checking tasks, including Geometric Attribute Classification (GAC), Geo-numeric Property Regression (GPR), and Spatial Predicate Prediction (SPP). The experimental results using several real-world large-scale geospatial datasets demonstrate that not only can LLMs recognize the geometric types of different data, but also achieves up to 73% accuracy on spatial predicate prediction tasks, while failing to give exact values for crucial geometric attributes such as area, centroid location, and distance in precise numeric values. Furthermore, the tasks of searching spatial related objects based on geographic descriptions are also quite poor. In a word, LLMs have mastered spatial syntax to a certain extent, but in the process of embedding different geometric data via WKT, various limitations such as loss of coordinate information due to fragmentation and tokens result in poor performances for Geo-Knowledge-Checking tasks and therefore, it is inevitable to incorporate the domain-specific geographic data and more advanced schemes to facilitate GeoAI researches in the future.
